meta.title: Fly Decision Help | Automated weather forecast analysis for paragliding takeoffs.
meta.description: Help for the Fly Decision web and app. Automated analysis of forecasts and orientations for paragliding takeoffs. Table with flight locations and hourly predictions for the upcoming days, featuring a 🟩🟨🟥 color-coded visual evaluation based on customizable safety limits.

header.subtitulo: Forecast, automated analysis, and paragliding takeoff maps

footer.texto: FlyDecision.com © Fly Decision 2026 | Changelog | Legal Notice & Privacy Policy

utilidad.respuesta:
To help you decide where to go flying.

It displays a table with your favorite takeoffs, their forecasts, and automatically analyzes those weather conditions.

It automatically performs a general part of the analysis you do when deciding to fly, cross-referencing general weather, precipitation, wind intensities, tolerable limits, and wind/takeoff orientations. With this color-coded or scored general information, you will still need to do your fine-tuned "human" analysis of the remaining conditions.

It has many more features and a takeoff map with interesting information and various filters (number of flights, average distance,...). You'll definitely want to explore it 😉.

ventajas.pregunta: ✨ What are the advantages that might interest me?
ventajas.respuesta:
✅ Quick assistance in choosing where to go flying, automatically sorting takeoffs by condition score. This allows you to filter in seconds which areas are worth looking at in detail and which ones to discard.

✅ 4-day forecast with hour-by-hour detail.

✅ Automatic forecast updates at the maximum frequency offered by original sources (8 times a day for Arome/Arpege and 4 times for ECMWF), with only about a 10-minute delay from their availability.

✅ Uses the statistically most reliable weather model for wind (Arome-HD 1.3km) in Southern Europe and for general weather (ECMWF IFS HRES 9km).

✅ Data filtering by time range (days/hours), recalculating the condition score for that specific interval.

✅ Takeoff filtering by minimum conditions or by distance to the takeoff.

✅ Search for takeoffs by name or province.

✅ Takeoff map, including many other possibilities: filters by number of flights registered in XContest, year of the last flight, minimum average distance flown, heatmap of over 1 million takeoff points (Iberian Peninsula, Alps, and Morocco), takeoffs from all over the world, etc.

✅ Links to other forecasts for each takeoff: Windy, Meteo-parapente, and Meteoblue, to have all data at hand.

✅ You can customize the limit values for average wind (minimum, ideal, and maximum) and maximum gust, so the system calculates the score adapted to your preferences.

✅ You can customize your usual schedule to select the day with one tap and directly see the score for that time range.

✅ Visual analysis line for Low-Level Wind Shear (LLWS) and also the degree of Reliability of the average wind forecast at 10m height.

precio.pregunta: 🆓 Is it free? Is personal data collected?
precio.respuesta:
Summary: It is free, and no personal or device data is collected.

  • No registration or subscription is required. The project's philosophy respects privacy: no advertising, no tracking cookies, no telemetry, etc.
  • Both the web access and the Android app are free to use and fully functional. While the app has some advantages, such as offline mode or full screen, it is not strictly necessary. Choose whichever you prefer as both work the same.
  • Your settings are saved privately in your browser or app.
  • We do not sell your data because, quite simply, we don't need it, we aren't interested in it, and we don't have it. This is an open free software project developed in collaboration with the community as a practical exercise in knowledge and entertainment. There's nothing hidden.
  • You can review all the original source code, download it, contribute, report bugs, or suggest features on our open project Fly Decision on GitHub.

software_libre.pregunta: 🕊️ Why is this project Free Software and not just Open Source?
software_libre.respuesta:
Summary: We chose Free Software as an ethical philosophy to protect user freedom and prevent collective work from being privatized.

  • You can review all the original source code, download it, contribute, report bugs, or suggest features on our open project Fly Decision on GitHub.
  • Two different philosophies: While Open Source has a purely practical focus to "make better software," Free Software is based on ethical and social values. It promotes solidarity, cooperation, and fundamental respect for user freedom.
  • The problem with permissive Open Source: Large corporations often prefer permissive open source licenses (like MIT) because the deal is: "Do whatever you want, even close the code and sell it." This allows heavily resourced companies to appropriate the community's work, privatize it, and profit abusively without returning improvements to the original creators.
  • The protection of Free Software (Copyleft): Free Software licenses (like the GPL family) say: "You are free to use and modify this code, but you cannot take that same freedom away from others." If someone modifies the code and distributes it, it must remain free.
  • Our commitment to AGPLv3: We use the GNU AGPLv3 license because it aligns best with equitable redistribution. It guarantees that this project will always belong to the people and can never be hijacked. If someone benefits from the community and improves it, they are legally obligated to return those improvements to the community.

android.respuesta:
Yes.

The website flydecision.com and the app work the same way. You can use whichever suits you best, although the app has the slight advantage of keeping data offline if you lose coverage and running in full screen.

iphone.respuesta:
While there is currently no native app in the App Store, you can install it as a Progressive Web App (PWA).

Although not exactly the same, a PWA is a web application that uses modern browser APIs to behave quite similarly to a native app.

The advantages of a PWA are that it displays in full screen, handles offline mode better, and starts up a bit faster.

To install it as a PWA:

  • Open the Fly Decision website in Safari.
  • Tap the "Share" button (the square with an upward arrow); depending on the version, you might have to scroll the menu to find the option.
  • Find and select "Add to Home Screen" and you'll have the icon on your desktop just like any other app.

ordenador.pregunta: 🖥️ Is there a desktop application (Windows or Mac)?
ordenador.respuesta:
There is no native desktop application, but it is available as a PWA (Progressive Web App). A PWA is a more "immersive" way to view it than on the web (no address bar or browser menus). It also behaves quite similarly to a desktop application.

To install it as a PWA:

  • Open the Fly Decision website in Chrome or Edge (PWAs are not currently supported in other browsers).
  • In the menu, look for the "Install app" option or similar, and it will be installed as an application. It might also be under options like "Send" or "Save".
  • To remove the application and return to "Web" mode, uninstall it just like any other application through your computer's settings.

meteo.pregunta: 📊 What weather data does it offer?
meteo.respuesta:
• It offers an hourly forecast for 4 days (96h).
  • The data includes: general weather icon (cloud cover/precipitation), precipitation, probability of precipitation, mean speed at 10m, max gust at 10m, direction at 10m, mean speed at 80, 120, and 180m, AGL Ceiling, CAPE, and CIN. The meters are referenced to the height above the takeoff ground level.
  • It updates automatically (Météo-France 8 times a day and ECMWF 4 times a day).
  • The original data comes from the public service provided by Météo-France and ECMWF. The wind weather models are Arome-HD 1.3km (for the first 0-48h), Arpege 7km (for the next 48-96h), and the rest of the weather uses ECMWF IFS HIRES 9km. The Open-Meteo.com gateway is used.
  • ⚠️ The essential cloud base data (CBH = Cloud Base Height) is currently missing to complete the takeoff weather. For now, you will have to check other weather services to see if the takeoff might be covered by clouds. This is a value offered by ECMWF but is not currently available on the weather gateway (we requested it in March 2026, but it is pending with no scheduled date).
  • It only analyzes forecasts for the specific takeoff points (not just anywhere) and covers Spain, Portugal, the Pyrenees (including the French side), and major Alps. It is an expanding project ⌛.

analisis.pregunta: 🤖 What kind of automatic analysis does it do?
analisis.respuesta:
• It analyzes the wind forecast and compares it with your configured mean wind and max gust limits, and with each takeoff's orientation. Internally, it processes an algorithm that approximates what you would do with that data, but for each takeoff, considering they have a fixed position and orientation(s).
  • It also checks the precipitation forecast for each hour.
  • With all the information, it scores each takeoff from 0 to 10 for the time range or days you choose. It also facilitates your manual analysis by automatically coloring 🟩 🟨 🟥 the data.
  • The internal algorithm analyzes the weather forecasts hour by hour to calculate a final score for the time range you select. If you select a different start or end time, the total score for that takeoff will change.
  • In Settings ⚙️ you can customize all those limits so the analysis takes your preferences into account, making the score more realistic for your desired conditions.
  • Additionally, it analyzes the difference in mean wind speeds at 10, 80, 120, and 180 m above ground level, offering a color-coded indicator for the degree of Shear and the Reliability of the 10m wind speed forecast.

algoritmo.pregunta: 🧑‍🎓 I want to know more about the automatic scoring algorithm
algoritmo.respuesta:
First, it is important to understand that claiming to "score" conditions from 0-10 with absolute reliability is very bold and pretentious, given the large number of factors involved, so this score must be understood as a "general overview" that will later have to be analyzed according to other circumstances. There are many limiting factors such as, of course, the variability of the predictions, the accuracy of the takeoff database and their orientations, the qualification of the suitability of the ideal orientation degree(s) of each takeoff, etc.

    Keeping all these limitations and many others in mind, let's see how the algorithm works:

  • Each hour has 100 possible points.
  • Direction is the most important factor (up to 50 points). It compares where the wind is coming from with the possible orientation(s) of the takeoff (there is much room for improvement here by qualifying with a weighted index of various ideal orientations in degrees, as some takeoffs offer a wide range of angles and others a narrow range). If the wind comes completely head-on (less than 10º deviation), it gives maximum points. As the wind crosses, the score drops and applies a penalty to the rest of the factors. Red line (Veto): If the wind comes with more than 120º deviation (extreme tail or crosswind), the hour is marked as "Not suitable" and receives 0 points.
  • Maximum gust (up to 30 points). If the gusts exceed your configured maximum limit, the points drop drastically and penalties are applied. Red line (Veto): If the gusts exceed your set maximum limit by 50%, it is considered dangerous and the hour receives 0 points.
  • Mean speed (Up to 20 points). It checks that the wind is strong enough, but not too strong. The "ideal" zone (between your configured minimum and maximum) grants 20 points. If the wind is light (less than the minimum) it gives 15 points. If it exceeds your maximum, the points drop quickly. Red line (Veto): If the mean speed doubles your maximum limit, the hour is blocked with 0 points.
  • Final calculation: if any "Veto" is triggered (due to precipitation, very bad direction, dangerous gust, or extreme speed), the final score for that hour is 0. If there are no vetos, the system adds the points (Direction + Gusts + Speed) and reduces them proportionally if the direction is slightly crossed or if the gust is somewhat strong. Finally, it accumulates the points of all valid hours to give you an overall score for that time range, normalized to values of 0-10.

dificultad.respuesta:
Yes. It is designed to be intuitive and simple. We recommend that you start without reading anything, just trying to use it by "tinkering". You won't break anything, and you can always restore it to the initial situation.

Once you have a general idea, you can consult the quick guides, which visually explain what each button on the two main screens does. If you have further doubts, you can always check this help page to fully grasp all the functionality.

You have several customization and configuration options that you can explore little by little, but they are not essential at the beginning.

basico.pregunta: 👉 Briefly, how does it work in "basic" normal use?
basico.respuesta:
• The first time you have to mark your favorite takeoffs. End of minimal configuration.
  • In normal use you tap the day of the week you want, and it shows you its hourly forecast and score.
  • Takeoffs are automatically sorted by their score, so by looking at the first ones you can already get an idea if it's possible to take off according to the conditions.

avanzado.respuesta:
• Time range selection: a 98h (4 days) time range selector is always visible. If you refine the time range for which you want to check the forecast, the condition score is automatically recalculated and, as always, the takeoffs are reordered by score.
  • Daily usual time range configuration: in Settings ⚙️ you can access several options, and one of them is setting the start and end time of your normal flying activity. This facilitates the automatic selection of that time range when you press the day of the week buttons.
  • Distance filter: Shows only takeoffs within the distance radius you select. This filter is available on the main screen and in the favorites editing screen. If you use it in favorites editing, it makes it easier to choose takeoffs near an unusual travel area and temporarily save them as favorites.
  • Customizing limits: in Settings ⚙️ you can change the default mean wind limits (minimum, ideal, and maximum) and max gusts. Your preferences will be used for the analysis, coloring, and scoring of conditions.
  • XC: AGL Ceiling, CAPE, and CIN: in Settings ⚙️ you can check a box to show data focused on Cross Country (XC) flying.
  • Shear and Reliability: in Settings ⚙️ you can check a box to show a colored row with the Low-Level Wind Shear level, which is a concurrent value with the Reliability of the 10m mean speed forecast.

soluciones.respuesta: • I have a phone with Android 13 and Xiaomi's MIUI 14 layer. It doesn't let me view it well, access Settings, or scroll the takeoff list: you need to install the latest version of Android System Webview from Google Play. After installation, open it and it will offer to use that version instead of the outdated one that came with your phone.

limitaciones.pregunta: 🛑 Current limitations to keep in mind
limitaciones.respuesta:
• Logically, meteorology is not an exact math, so scoring conditions from 0-10, with the level of variables involved, must be assumed as a "general clue".
  • For now, it only shows and analyzes weather at takeoff locations. It does not analyze all other meteorological factors of the takeoff or the flight itself.
  • Takeoffs have one or more orientations, and some angles are more perfect than others for taking off. Currently, all orientations have equal value. There is a large possibility (and work) for improvement here, indicating the weighted value of the possible angles to compare it with the angular difference with the forecast.
  • Can it be expanded to other areas, with more weather data...? Yes and no. The gateway used to obtain weather data has server call limits. There are subscription levels that uncap access, but the idea is to keep the project free with manageable expenses and not use advertising, subscriptions, grants, sponsorships, or anything related to expenses and complications.
  • ⚠️ The essential cloud base data (CBH = Cloud Base Height) is currently missing from the forecast to complete the weather at takeoff. For now, you will have to consult other weather services to see if the takeoff might be covered by clouds. This is a value offered by ECMWF but is not currently available on the weather gateway (we requested it in March 2026, but it is pending with no scheduled date).

desarrollo.respuesta:
• To offer Alpine takeoffs, the most reliable model in that area needs to be added: ICON-D2. Mixing models is complex and, furthermore, there are the aforementioned economic limitations of the weather gateway, so this topic is under study.
  • Add a time range filter to the map so takeoffs are automatically colored based on conditions. Also add a condition filter (0-10).
  • Add takeoffs from the rest of France, Portugal, and the Alps.
  • The possibility of obtaining this same data but for any point, not just for the fixed takeoff points available in the database. Actually, this is already implemented internally but it's not possible to make it public as it would clash with the limitations of the free Open-Meteo service mentioned above.

responsabilidad.respuesta:
Summary: This application is an informative support tool. Weather forecasts can fail, condition scoring can be incorrect, and the pilot is the only person responsible for evaluating real conditions and making the final decision before flying.

How does the system work? (Intended use)

  • Fly Decision is a support tool for personal final decision-making, offering the forecast and performing an automatic analysis of general conditions for paragliding takeoff areas.
  • The system evaluates predicted parameters (such as precipitation, wind speed, gusts, and direction) by comparing them with configurable safety limits defined by you.
  • It offers hourly predictions and classifies each time slot using a visual color scale for easy at-a-glance understanding.

🛑 Disclaimer

  • For informational purposes only: This software is provided exclusively for informational purposes and under no circumstances should it be used as a flight safety system or as the sole basis for making decisions affecting your safety.
  • Forecast limitations: Weather data comes from numerical models. These forecasts may contain significant errors and may not correctly represent actual local effects or the aerology of the area.
  • Exclusive responsibility of the pilot, who must evaluate the actual and current conditions at the takeoff site and make the final decision on whether it is safe to fly or not.

utilidad.respuesta:
For flights in your usual areas:

  • Quickly check the weather (Windy, Meteo-parapente, and Meteoblue) by searching for your usual takeoff.
  • View only the takeoffs that match a certain wind orientation using the filters.

For trips and new areas:

  • Plan trips and locate active flight zones.
  • Find out if a place is operational or abandoned by filtering by the number of recent flights.
  • Find exact points and takeoff coordinates.
  • Discover alternative takeoffs using the 'Heatmap' layer.
  • Facilitate navigation by GPS or on foot to the takeoff through links to Google Maps or Brouter.

despegues.respuesta:
The map initially shows the 'Takeoffs' layer. Points are grouped into numerical circles when zooming out. Click on them or zoom in to see individual takeoffs.

When you tap on a takeoff, you will see basic information, orientations, and direct links to the weather forecast.

In 'More information' you will find coordinates, links to topographic maps, XContest flights, and potential warnings or restrictions.

Search: Use the '🔍 Takeoff' box to search by name. When you select one from the list, the map will take you directly to it.

Activity level: Each takeoff has a colored circle indicating when it was last flown (based on XContest data).

  • 🟢 Green: Normal (flights in the last 6 months).
  • 🟠 Orange: Medium (flights 6-12 months ago).
  • 🟡 Yellow: Low (flights 12-24 months ago).
  • ⚪ White: Probably inactive (more than 2 years without records).

capas.respuesta:
By activating the 'Layers and filters' button, you can show or hide different information layers on the map. You can keep this panel fixed using the pin button (📌).

Initially, only the 'Takeoffs' layer is active. However, you can activate the special 'Paraglidingearth Takeoffs' layer.

Note on ParaglidingEarth: This is unrefined and uncorrected data imported from their database. They are shown in gray because they may contain errors and are not indexed in the main search engine, but they are very useful for discovering new areas when traveling abroad.

calor.respuesta:
The heatmap allows you to visualize thousands of real takeoff points recorded in XContest over 20 years. They are shown in a gradient: from yellow (few flights) to intense red (high flight density).

These are exact takeoff points recorded in XContest from October 2006 to May 2025. They are divided into three layers: Iberian Peninsula (about 300,000 points), Alps (about 950,000 points), and Morocco (about 15,000 points).

Example: In the Arcones/Somosierra area, we see the 2 main flight zones. In this case, for greater clarity, the 'Takeoffs' layer is deactivated and, to illustrate the possibility, it is shown with the 'IGN Spain topographic' background layer (changeable in the 'Layers' button at the top right).

Going further into detail, we can zoom into the Somosierra takeoff area, set aerial images as the background, and thus identify the 2 most used areas in that zone:

⚠️ Warning: These points are not official takeoffs. They include records started in flight or track errors (people who turned on the GPS in the car). However, they are a very useful tool for discovering alternative takeoffs in a known area or finding real takeoffs when traveling to a new area.

filtros.pregunta: 🔍 Orientation and number of flights filters
filtros.respuesta:
Wind filter: You can choose to have the map show only the takeoffs that match the wind orientation given by the forecasts for that day.

Popularity / activity filter: Using the 'Min. number of flights' and 'Year of last flight' filters, you can hide irrelevant takeoffs. For example, if you are traveling to the Alps and only want to see the main and active takeoffs, you can set 'Min. flights: 3000'.

tipos.respuesta:
In the control located at the top right, you can change the base layer of the map. You have options such as topographic maps, satellite aerial images, thermal heatmaps, and paragliding XC routes, among others, which is very useful for planning flights or approaches (Hike & Fly).

On the left of the screen, you have a button to center the map on your GPS location and another to search for any town, place, or coordinates.

configuracion.respuesta:
By clicking the gear icon (top right), you access the settings. Here you can set the default values you want for the 'Min. number of flights' and 'Year of last flight' filters every time you open the application.

These settings are automatically saved in your browser or device. You can temporarily modify them in the normal 'Layers and filters' panel, but when the page is reloaded, they will return to the values you set here.
